prior.content_md:
## The Regulation

Regulation (EU) 2024/1257 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 24 April 2024 establishes the next generation of EU emission standards (Euro 7) for type-approval of motor vehicles and engines. It was published in the Official Journal on 8 May 2024 and entered into force on 28 May 2024.

*Source: Regulation (EU) 2024/1257, Official Journal of the European Union, 8 May 2024. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/eli/reg/2024/1257/oj/eng. Tier 1 — binding EU law.*

For the first time, Euro 7 consolidates light-duty and heavy-duty emission standards into a single legislative instrument. Previous Euro standards addressed these vehicle categories under separate frameworks. The unified regulation covers exhaust emissions (NOx, particulate matter, hydrocarbons, CO), brake particle emissions, and tyre particle emissions — the latter two being regulated at the EU vehicle level for the first time.

*Source: Regulation (EU) 2024/1257, recitals and Articles 1–3. Tier 1 — binding EU law.*

## Why It Applies to the Workspace

The workspace, operating as a freight forwarder on European trade lanes, does not typically hold vehicle type-approval obligations. undefined undefined However, Euro 7 applies to the workspace in two indirect but operationally material ways:

**First, road carrier dependency.** The workspace's road-mode operations rely on third-party road carriers whose fleets will be subject to Euro 7 compliance requirements. Carriers unable or unwilling to invest in compliant vehicles face type-approval restrictions that will progressively remove non-compliant new vehicles from the market. The workspace is a buyer of road capacity: as compliant vehicle supply changes, road transport cost structures for European lanes will shift.

**Second, high-value and time-critical cargo characteristics.** The workspace's verticals — live events, fine art, luxury goods, film and TV, high-value automotive, humanitarian — disproportionately depend on reliable, specialist road carriers for European final-mile and inter-modal transfer legs. These are typically smaller, specialist operators who may face higher per-unit compliance costs than large fleet operators, with corresponding pressure on surcharge structures.

*Source: Regulation (EU) 2024/1257, Articles 1–4, scope provisions. Tier 1 — binding EU law.*
